questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A vertical shaft is generally the preferred means of access when the deposit is
options: very shallow and outcropping on a hillside
deep, requiring high-capacity, high-speed hoisting of men and minerals
a thin placer in a river bed
exposed at the surface over a wide area
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Access type is chosen on depth, dip and required hoisting capacity.

**Reasoning.** For deep deposits a vertical shaft gives the shortest, most direct route and supports fast, high-capacity skip/cage hoisting that inclines or adits cannot match.

**Answer.** Deep deposit needing high-capacity hoisting.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An adit is
options: a vertical opening to the surface
a near-horizontal drivage from the surface into a hillside, also giving gravity drainage
a blind raise between two levels
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Adits exploit topography.

**Reasoning.** Where a deposit lies in a hill, a horizontal (or slightly rising) adit driven from the hillside reaches the orebody directly and lets water drain out by gravity, avoiding pumping.

**Answer.** Near-horizontal drivage into a hillside with gravity drainage.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A decline (inclined drift) is often preferred over a shaft when
options: the deposit is extremely deep (>1500 m)
trackless mobile equipment (LHDs, trucks) must drive directly in and out of the mine
no mineral is to be hoisted
the rock is too weak to support any opening
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Declines suit rubber-tyred, trackless operations.

**Reasoning.** A spiral/straight decline lets LHDs and trucks travel between surface and workings under their own power, ideal at moderate depth; shafts force transfer to hoists.

**Answer.** When trackless mobile equipment must drive in and out.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The two stages of bord-and-pillar (room-and-pillar) coal mining are
options: development (driving bords, leaving pillars) and depillaring (extracting the pillars)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bord-and-pillar works in two passes.

**Reasoning.** First, galleries (bords) are driven on a grid leaving coal pillars that support the roof (development/first working); later the pillars themselves are extracted on retreat (depillaring/second working).

**Answer.** Development and depillaring.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Square pillars 20 m across are formed on 25 m centres (5 m wide galleries). The extraction ratio during development is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Extraction ratio $e=1-\dfrac{w^2}{(w+B)^2}$ for square pillars on square centres.

**Working.**
$$e=1-\frac{20^2}{25^2}=1-\frac{400}{625}=1-0.64=0.36$$

**Answer.** 0.36.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the same layout, the fraction of coal left in pillars after development is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal remaining $=1-e$.

**Working.**
$$1-0.36=0.64$$

**Answer.** 0.64.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Percentage extraction during the development (first working) stage of bord-and-pillar is low because
options: the coal seam is very thin
most of the coal is left in pillars to support the roof
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillars carry the overburden load during development.

**Reasoning.** Only the gallery coal is won initially; the bulk stays in standing pillars to keep the roof up, so first-working extraction is typically 25–40%.

**Answer.** Most coal is locked in support pillars.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Depillaring (second working) is normally carried out
options: advancing away from the shaft, leaving the goaf ahead
retreating toward the shaft, allowing the roof to cave into the goaf behind
without any roof support at all
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillar extraction is done in retreat for safety.

**Reasoning.** Pillars are split and lifted while the line of extraction retreats toward the shaft; the de-stressed roof caves into the goaf behind the working line, keeping fresh workings ahead of the caved zone.

**Answer.** Retreating toward the shaft, caving behind.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: At a depth of 200 m the average overburden unit weight is 25 kN/m³. The vertical (overburden) stress, in MPa, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_v=\gamma H$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_v=25\times200=5000\ \text{kPa}=5\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 5 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using tributary area theory with a vertical stress of 5 MPa and an extraction ratio of 0.36, the average pillar stress, in MPa, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_p=\dfrac{\sigma_v}{1-e}$ (tributary area theory).

**Working.**
$$\sigma_p=\frac{5}{1-0.36}=\frac{5}{0.64}=7.81\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 7.81 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: If the overburden unit weight is 25 kN/m³, the depth at which the vertical stress is 5 MPa, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $H=\dfrac{\sigma_v}{\gamma}$.

**Working.**
$$H=\frac{5000}{25}=200\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 200 m.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Tributary area theory for pillar stress assumes that
options: the roof carries all the load and the pillar none
each pillar supports the full overburden weight over its share of area (pillar plus surrounding openings)
the pillar stress equals the vertical in-situ stress regardless of extraction
stress is uniform across the whole panel including the goaf
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Load is shared on a per-pillar basis.

**Reasoning.** The theory allots to each pillar the full column of overburden over the unit cell (pillar + half the adjacent galleries), giving $\sigma_p=\sigma_v/(1-e)$. It is conservative as it ignores any arching/abutment relief.

**Answer.** Each pillar carries the overburden over its tributary area.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: As the development extraction ratio increases (pillars made smaller), the average pillar stress
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_p=\sigma_v/(1-e)$.

**Reasoning.** A higher $e$ means a smaller pillar area carrying the same overburden, so the stress on the remaining pillars rises.

**Answer.** Increases.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using the Obert-Duvall formula Sp = S1(0.778 + 0.222 w/h) with cube strength S1 = 7 MPa, pillar width w = 20 m and height h = 4 m, the pillar strength, in MPa, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Obert-Duvall pillar strength: $S_p=S_1(0.778+0.222\,w/h)$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{w}{h}=\frac{20}{4}=5$$
$$S_p=7\,(0.778+0.222\times5)=7\times1.888=13.22\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 13.22 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For a pillar strength of 13.22 MPa and a pillar stress of 7.81 MPa, the factor of safety is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FS=\dfrac{\text{pillar strength}}{\text{pillar stress}}$.

**Working.**
$$FS=\frac{13.22}{7.81}=1.69$$

**Answer.** 1.69.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: As the width-to-height (w/h) ratio of a pillar increases, the pillar becomes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillar strength formulas increase with $w/h$.

**Reasoning.** A squatter pillar confines its core, raising the triaxial strength of the central material; slender pillars (low $w/h$) fail more readily.

**Answer.** Stronger as w/h increases.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A pillar whose factor of safety falls below 1.0 is expected to
options: yield or fail under the imposed load
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FS<1$ means demand exceeds capacity.

**Reasoning.** When the pillar stress exceeds its strength, the pillar yields/crushes, possibly triggering load transfer and progressive failure.

**Answer.** It yields/fails.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using the Bieniawski formula Sp = S1(0.64 + 0.36 w/h) with S1 = 5 MPa and w/h = 5, the pillar strength, in MPa, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bieniawski pillar strength: $S_p=S_1(0.64+0.36\,w/h)$.

**Working.**
$$S_p=5\,(0.64+0.36\times5)=5\times2.44=12.2\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 12.2 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The size (scale) effect on rock/coal strength means that, up to a critical size, larger specimens are
options: weaker than small specimens because they contain more flaws
of identical strength to small specimens
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strength decreases with specimen size (Gaddy/Holland).

**Reasoning.** Larger volumes statistically contain more and larger flaws, lowering strength until a critical size is reached beyond which strength is roughly constant. Cube-strength formulas correct for this.

**Answer.** Weaker (more flaws).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A 500 m × 500 m panel is laid out with pillars on 25 m centres. The number of pillars in the panel is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillars per side $=\dfrac{\text{panel dimension}}{\text{centre spacing}}$.

**Working.**
$$\left(\frac{500}{25}\right)^2=20\times20=400$$

**Answer.** 400 pillars.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Longwall mining extracts the seam by
options: leaving large permanent pillars between rooms
winning the full seam height along a long face in a single pass, the roof caving behind the supports
augering into the highwall from the surface
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Longwall is a near-total-extraction caving method.

**Reasoning.** A long face (100–300 m) is mined in slices; powered supports hold the immediate roof at the face and the strata cave into the goaf as the face advances, giving very high recovery.

**Answer.** Full-seam single-pass face with caving behind.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In retreating longwall mining,
options: the face advances away from the shaft into virgin ground
gate roads are first driven to the panel boundary and the face then retreats toward the shaft
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Retreat mining develops the boundary first.

**Reasoning.** Both gate roads are driven out to the far end of the panel in solid coal; the face is then installed at the boundary and retreats back toward the shaft, leaving caved goaf behind in already-developed ground.

**Answer.** Develop to boundary, then retreat toward the shaft.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A key advantage of longwall over bord-and-pillar mining is
options: very high percentage extraction (80–90%) with concentrated, high output
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Longwall maximises recovery.

**Reasoning.** Because pillars are not left in the panel, recovery reaches 80–90% with high, concentrated production from a single face — at the cost of high capital equipment and planned surface subsidence.

**Answer.** High percentage extraction and concentrated output.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A longwall face is 200 m long and 2 m high; the shearer takes a 0.8 m web and makes 10 cuts per day. Coal density is 1.4 t/m³. The daily production, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ face length $\times$ web $\times$ height $\times$ density $\times$ cuts/day.

**Working.**
$$Q=200\times0.8\times2\times1.4\times10=4480\ \text{t/day}$$

**Answer.** 4480 t/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the same face, the coal produced per shearer cut, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Per-cut tonnage $=$ face length $\times$ web $\times$ height $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$Q=200\times0.8\times2\times1.4=448\ \text{t/cut}$$

**Answer.** 448 t.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Powered roof supports (shield/chock supports) on a longwall face
options: support the roof at the face and self-advance, allowing the roof to cave behind them
are used only in metal mines
replace the need for a shearer
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The supports protect the working space and march with the face.

**Reasoning.** Hydraulic shields hold the immediate roof over the face conveyor and walkway; after each cut they lower, advance and reset, deliberately letting the unsupported roof behind cave into the goaf.

**Answer.** Support the face roof and self-advance, caving behind.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The 'goaf' (gob) in longwall mining is
options: the solid coal ahead of the face
the caved/void zone left behind the advancing supports after extraction
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The goaf is the mined-out space.

**Reasoning.** As the face advances and supports move forward, the roof behind collapses into the extracted void — this caved region is the goaf, which must be managed for spontaneous heating and gas.

**Answer.** The caved void behind the supports.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: With a 0.8 m web and 10 cuts per day, the longwall face advance, in m/day, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Advance $=$ web $\times$ cuts/day.

**Working.**
$$a=0.8\times10=8\ \text{m/day}$$

**Answer.** 8 m/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A longwall panel is 1500 m long and the face advances 8 m/day. The time to mine out the panel, in days, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time $=\dfrac{\text{panel length}}{\text{advance rate}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{1500}{8}=187.5\ \text{days}$$

**Answer.** 187.5 days.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Compared with bord-and-pillar mining with standing pillars, surface subsidence over a longwall panel is
options: negligible and never reaches the surface
immediate and follows the face, forming a predictable subsidence trough
delayed by decades and always uncertain
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total extraction with caving causes prompt subsidence.

**Reasoning.** As the roof caves behind a longwall face the overburden settles almost immediately, producing a travelling, predictable subsidence trough — unlike bord-and-pillar where standing pillars may delay or render subsidence uncertain.

**Answer.** Immediate, predictable subsidence trough following the face.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: At 240 m depth with an overburden unit weight of 25 kN/m³, the vertical stress, in MPa, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_v=\gamma H$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_v=25\times240=6000\ \text{kPa}=6\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 6 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Square pillars 15 m wide are formed with 5 m galleries (20 m centres). The development extraction ratio is ____ (four decimals).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using tributary area theory with σv = 6 MPa and e = 0.4375, the average pillar stress, in MPa, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_p=\dfrac{\sigma_v}{1-e}$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_p=\frac{6}{0.5625}=10.67\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** 10.67 MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In the plan shown, square pillars (w = 15 m) are separated by 5 m galleries (B). The tributary area assigned to each pillar, in m², is ____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='360' height='220' fill='#ffffff'/><g fill='#9ca3af' stroke='#1f2937'><rect x='40' y='30' width='60' height='60'/><rect x='120' y='30' width='60' height='60'/><rect x='200' y='30' width='60' height='60'/><rect x='40' y='110' width='60' height='60'/><rect x='120' y='110' width='60' height='60'/><rect x='200' y='110' width='60' height='60'/></g><rect x='110' y='20' width='80' height='80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><text x='66' y='64' font-size='12' fill='#111111'>w</text><text x='104' y='106' font-size='10' fill='#111111'>B</text><text x='96' y='205' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626'>tributary cell = (w+B)^2</text></svg>
figure.caption: Plan of square pillars on regular centres; the dashed cell (pillar + surrounding half-galleries) is the tributary area.

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tributary area $=(w+B)^2$.

**Working.**
$$A=(15+5)^2=20^2=400\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** 400 m².

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using Obert-Duvall Sp = S1(0.778 + 0.222 w/h) with S1 = 8 MPa, w = 15 m and h = 3 m, the pillar strength, in MPa, is ____ (one decimal).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For a pillar strength of 15.1 MPa and a pillar stress of 10.67 MPa, the factor of safety is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FS=\dfrac{S_p}{\sigma_p}$.

**Working.**
$$FS=\frac{15.1}{10.67}=1.42$$

**Answer.** 1.42.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: To raise the factor of safety of pillars in a deepening mine, one can
options: narrow the pillars and widen the galleries
enlarge the pillars (lower the extraction ratio), reducing pillar stress and raising w/h
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FS$ rises with strength and falls with stress.

**Reasoning.** Larger pillars lower $e$ (so $\sigma_p=\sigma_v/(1-e)$ falls) and raise $w/h$ (so $S_p$ rises) — both increase $FS$. The penalty is lower recovery.

**Answer.** Enlarge pillars / reduce extraction ratio.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A 'stook' (snook) left during depillaring is
options: a small remnant of pillar left temporarily to support the roof while the pillar is being split/lifted
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillars are reduced in stages during extraction.

**Reasoning.** A stook is the small final remnant of a pillar kept momentarily to hold the roof until the goaf is ready to cave; it is then blasted/withdrawn under controlled retreat.

**Answer.** A small temporary pillar remnant during lifting.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The armoured face conveyor (AFC) on a longwall
options: supports the roof at the face
carries coal along the face and provides the haulage track/guide for the shearer
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The AFC is the face transport spine.

**Reasoning.** It runs the full face length carrying cut coal to the maingate, and the shearer hauls itself along the AFC's rack/guide; it is also pushed over by the supports to advance the face.

**Answer.** Conveys coal and guides the shearer along the face.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The beam stage loader (BSL) on a longwall
options: is the roof support at the tailgate
transfers coal from the AFC to the maingate belt conveyor, often incorporating a crusher
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The BSL links the face conveyor to the gate belt.

**Reasoning.** At the maingate the BSL receives coal from the right-angle AFC discharge, sizes it through a crusher, and loads it onto the gate (trunk) conveyor for transport out.

**Answer.** Transfers/crushes coal from AFC to the maingate belt.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A shearer traverses a 200 m face at a haulage (cutting) speed of 6 m/min. The time for one full cut, in minutes, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time $=\dfrac{\text{face length}}{\text{cutting speed}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{200}{6}=33.33\ \text{min}$$

**Answer.** 33.33 min.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: With a 0.8 m web, 2 m cutting height, 6 m/min cutting speed and 1.4 t/m³ coal density, the instantaneous coal-cutting rate, in t/min, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rate $=$ web $\times$ height $\times$ speed $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$\dot Q=0.8\times2\times6\times1.4=13.44\ \text{t/min}$$

**Answer.** 13.44 t/min.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Expressed per hour, the instantaneous cutting rate of 13.44 t/min is ____ t/h.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Multiply per-minute rate by 60.

**Working.**
$$\dot Q=13.44\times60=806.4\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 806.4 t/h.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Periodic 'weighting' on a longwall face refers to
options: the daily tonnage weighed at the surface
the cyclic increase in support load as the main roof spans, breaks and caves at regular intervals
the weight of the shearer drum
the mass of explosives used per round
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The main roof caves in periodic steps behind the face.

**Reasoning.** As the face advances, the main roof cantilever lengthens until it breaks; just before each break the supports feel a load surge (weighting). The first (main) fall occurs at a larger span; subsequent falls recur at the periodic weighting interval.

**Answer.** Cyclic support-load surges as the main roof breaks and caves.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A 200 m longwall face is equipped with powered supports spaced at 2 m. The number of supports on the face is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number $=\dfrac{\text{face length}}{\text{support spacing}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{200}{2}=100$$

**Answer.** 100 supports.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The risk of spontaneous heating (fire) in a longwall goaf is greatest when
options: the coal has low proneness and the goaf is air-tight
the coal is highly prone to oxidation and air leaks slowly through the goaf
the panel is fully stowed with sand
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Spontaneous heating needs reactive coal plus a slow air supply.

**Reasoning.** Crushed, oxidation-prone coal in the goaf generates heat; if air leaks through at a rate fast enough to supply oxygen but too slow to carry heat away, temperature builds to ignition. Sealing or flooding the goaf reduces the risk.

**Answer.** Prone coal with slow air leakage through the goaf.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Longwall mining is best suited to
options: highly faulted, irregular, steeply pitching seams
fairly regular, large, undisturbed seams of moderate, uniform thickness
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Longwall equipment is expensive and inflexible.

**Reasoning.** Its economics depend on long, uninterrupted runs, so it favours large, regular, gently dipping seams of fairly uniform thickness free of major faults and washouts.

**Answer.** Regular, large, undisturbed seams of uniform thickness.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Hydraulic sand stowing of the goaf is practised primarily to
options: fill the void to control surface subsidence and support the roof instead of caving
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stowing replaces caving with a solid fill.

**Reasoning.** Sand transported hydraulically fills the mined-out space, limiting roof convergence and surface subsidence — important under surface structures or water bodies — while also reducing fire risk in the goaf.

**Answer.** Fill the goaf to control subsidence and support the roof.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: On a face, the main (first) roof fall occurs at 30 m of advance and periodic weightings recur every 15 m thereafter. The number of periodic weightings during the first 150 m of advance is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** After the main fall, count the periodic intervals over the remaining advance.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{150-30}{15}=\frac{120}{15}=8$$

**Answer.** 8 periodic weightings.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The 'web' of a longwall shearer is
options: the height of the seam being cut
the depth (thickness) of coal sliced off the face in one pass
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The web is the horizontal cut depth.

**Reasoning.** Each shearer traverse removes a slice of coal of thickness equal to the web (typically 0.6–1.0 m); face advance per cut equals the web.

**Answer.** Depth of coal removed per pass.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Rock bolts support a mine roof mainly by
options: adding dead weight to the roof
reinforcing the strata — suspending weak beds from strong strata and binding beds into a composite beam
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bolts are active reinforcement, not passive props.

**Reasoning.** By the suspension mechanism they hang weak immediate roof from a competent bed; by the beam-building mechanism they clamp thin laminations into a thicker, stiffer beam — both reduce sag and improve roof stability.

**Answer.** Reinforce strata (suspension / beam-building).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A rock bolt of 22 mm diameter is made of steel with a yield strength of 400 MPa. Its yield load, in kN, is ____ (nearest whole number).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Yield load $=$ yield stress $\times$ cross-sectional area.

**Working.**
$$A=\frac{\pi}{4}(22)^2=380.1\ \text{mm}^2$$
$$P=400\times380.1=152{,}040\ \text{N}\approx152\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 152 kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A 1 m thick loose roof bed (unit weight 25 kN/m³) is to be suspended by bolts each able to carry 150 kN. For a square bolt pattern, the maximum bolt spacing, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each bolt carries the dead weight of its tributary roof area.

**Working.** Roof load per unit area $=\gamma t=25\times1=25\ \text{kN/m}^2$. Area per bolt $=\dfrac{150}{25}=6\ \text{m}^2$.
$$s=\sqrt{6}=2.45\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 2.45 m.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Yielding steel arch (ring) supports are most appropriate for
options: permanent main shafts in hard rock
roadways in weak, squeezing ground where some controlled convergence must be accommodated
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Some ground deforms too much for rigid support.

**Reasoning.** In squeezing/weak strata, sliding-joint steel arches yield (shorten) under load, releasing strain energy gradually instead of failing brittlely, maintaining the roadway profile.

**Answer.** Roadways in weak, squeezing ground.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A crib (chock) in underground support practice is
options: a stacked timber/steel framework used to support the roof, e.g. at junctions or the goaf edge
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cribs are built-up standing supports.

**Reasoning.** Layers of timber or steel are interlocked into a square crib that carries high loads where extra standing support is needed, such as gate-road ends and breaker lines.

**Answer.** A stacked framework standing support.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: 'Convergence' of an underground roadway means
options: the airflow meeting at a junction
the reduction in roof-to-floor (or rib-to-rib) distance under stress
the meeting of two development drivages
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stress closes the opening.

**Reasoning.** Convergence is the measured closure of an opening — roof sag plus floor heave (or rib squeeze) — used to monitor strata behaviour and support performance.

**Answer.** Reduction in roof-floor / rib-rib distance.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A 3.5 m high roadway undergoes 7% vertical convergence. The roof-to-floor closure, in mm, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closure $=$ convergence fraction $\times$ original height.

**Working.**
$$\Delta=0.07\times3500=245\ \text{mm}$$

**Answer.** 245 mm.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Yield pillars are deliberately designed to
options: yield and transfer load to stiffer abutments, controlling stress around gate roads
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sometimes it is better for a pillar to fail softly.

**Reasoning.** Small yield pillars are sized to crush in a controlled manner, shedding load to solid abutments and avoiding sudden stress concentration/bumps near roadways — a strategy used in some longwall gate-road designs.

**Answer.** Yield to shed load to abutments.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The principal purpose of a barrier pillar is to
options: isolate panels/old workings (against water, gas and stress) and protect main roadways
support the headframe at the surface
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Barrier pillars are large protective separators.

**Reasoning.** Left between panels or around mains, they prevent inrush of water/gas from old workings, shield main roadways from panel abutment stress, and limit propagation of failures.

**Answer.** Isolate workings and protect mains.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A longwall block contains 5 Mt of coal in situ and the overall extraction (recovery) is 85%. The recoverable coal, in Mt, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Recoverable $=$ in-situ $\times$ recovery.

**Working.**
$$R=5\times0.85=4.25\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 4.25 Mt.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Underground metal mining methods are broadly classified as
options: unsupported (naturally supported), supported, and caving methods
shallow, medium and deep methods only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The classic classification is by ground-support strategy.

**Reasoning.** Methods are grouped as naturally supported/open (e.g. sublevel and room-and-pillar), artificially supported (e.g. cut-and-fill, square-set) and caving (e.g. block and sublevel caving).

**Answer.** Unsupported, supported and caving methods.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Sublevel (open) stoping is best suited to an orebody that is
options: weak, with weak walls and irregular shape
strong, with strong walls, steeply dipping and fairly regular
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Open stoping leaves large unsupported voids during mining.

**Reasoning.** This is only safe where both ore and wall rock are strong and the orebody is steep and regular, so the open stope stands until backfilled or left.

**Answer.** Strong ore and walls, steep and regular.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In shrinkage stoping,
options: the stope is filled with imported backfill after each slice
part of the broken ore is left in the stope as a working platform and temporary wall support, only fully drawn at the end
the ore is drawn completely after each blast
no ore is broken until the stope is complete
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The broken muck pile itself is the support and floor.

**Reasoning.** Miners work upward standing on the broken ore; because broken ore swells, a portion (~one-third) is drawn after each blast to keep working height, and the remaining ~two-thirds is drawn only after the stope is fully mined.

**Answer.** Broken ore retained as platform/support, drawn at the end.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Ore swells by 50% when broken. The fraction of broken ore that must be drawn off after each blast to maintain working height in a shrinkage stope is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The drawn fraction equals the swell expressed over the loose volume: $\dfrac{\text{swell}}{1+\text{swell}}$.

**Working.**
$$f=\frac{0.5}{1.5}=0.33$$

**Answer.** 0.33 (about one-third).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Cut-and-fill stoping mines the ore
options: in a single large open void left permanently
in successive (usually horizontal) slices, each mined slice being replaced with backfill that supports the walls and forms the next working floor
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cut-and-fill alternates extraction with filling.

**Reasoning.** A slice of ore is mined, then the void is filled (hydraulic/paste/rock fill); the fill supports the walls, controls subsidence and gives a platform to mine the next slice. It suits weak walls and high-grade, irregular orebodies.

**Answer.** Slice-by-slice mining with backfill between slices.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Backfill placed in a cut-and-fill stope primarily
options: supports the walls, provides a working platform and improves regional ground stability
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fill is a structural element of the method.

**Reasoning.** It confines the wall rock (raising its strength), gives a stable floor for the next lift, and limits convergence/subsidence across the mine. Cemented fill can even stand as a free wall for adjacent extraction.

**Answer.** Wall support, platform and regional stability.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A cemented backfill of 1000 t is mixed to a 5% cement content by mass. The cement required, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cement mass $=$ fill mass $\times$ cement fraction.

**Working.**
$$m=1000\times0.05=50\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 50 t.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Square-set stoping is characterised by
options: a self-supporting open stope in strong rock
a complete interlocking timber framework supporting very weak ore and walls (high-grade, irregular, but costly)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Square-setting builds a structural skeleton as it mines.

**Reasoning.** Interlocking timber 'sets' fill the mined space, supporting extremely weak ground for high-grade, irregular orebodies. It is labour- and timber-intensive and now largely superseded by cut-and-fill.

**Answer.** Interlocking timber framework for very weak, high-grade ground.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Block caving is most suited to
options: large, low-grade, massive orebodies that cave readily under their own weight once undercut
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Caving substitutes gravity for drilling/blasting of the ore column.

**Reasoning.** A massive, fairly low-grade orebody is undercut so the block caves and fragments under gravity, then is drawn from below. The very low cost per tonne offsets the low grade; surface subsidence must be acceptable.

**Answer.** Large, low-grade, massive, caveable orebodies.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The single most critical requirement for successful block caving is that the ore
options: is extremely hard and massive with no joints
caves and fragments readily once the support is removed by undercutting
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Caving relies on the rock breaking itself.

**Reasoning.** Adequate caveability — governed by jointing, strength and stress — ensures the block propagates upward and fragments to a drawable size after undercutting. Massive, unjointed ore may not cave or may produce oversize.

**Answer.** It must cave/fragment readily after undercutting.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A caving column is 100 m high. If 80% of the column is drawn as ore before dilution becomes excessive, the ore-column height drawn, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Drawn height $=$ draw fraction $\times$ column height.

**Working.**
$$h=0.8\times100=80\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 80 m.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In sublevel caving, the ore is
options: mined in open stopes with no caving
blasted by ring drilling on sublevels and drawn beneath a cap of caved waste, so dilution from waste is inherent
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sublevel caving caves the waste, not the ore.

**Reasoning.** Rings of upward holes are drilled and blasted on sublevels; the ore is drawn under an overlying mat of caved waste that follows it down — so some waste dilution is unavoidable and draw control is essential.

**Answer.** Ring-blasted ore drawn under caved waste (inherent dilution).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: 1000 t of ore is drawn together with 100 t of waste rock. The dilution, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dilution $=\dfrac{\text{waste tonnes}}{\text{ore tonnes}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$D=\frac{100}{1000}\times100=10\%$$

**Answer.** 10%.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: 1000 t of ore at 5% metal is diluted by 100 t of barren (0%) waste. The diluted (delivered) grade, in %, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Diluted grade $=\dfrac{\text{metal mass}}{\text{total mass}}$.

**Working.**
$$g=\frac{1000\times5}{1000+100}=\frac{5000}{1100}=4.55\%$$

**Answer.** 4.55%.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Of 1000 t of ore in situ, 950 t is recovered. The mining recovery, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Recovery $=\dfrac{\text{ore recovered}}{\text{ore in situ}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$R=\frac{950}{1000}\times100=95\%$$

**Answer.** 95%.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Vertical crater retreat (VCR) mining breaks ore by
options: spherical (crater) charges placed in large-diameter downholes, blasting horizontal slices that retreat upward, ore drawn from below
questions.solution:
**Concept.** VCR applies crater-blasting theory to large downholes.

**Reasoning.** Concentrated spherical charges at optimum depth crater the ore into horizontal slabs; the stope retreats upward, broken ore being drawn from drawpoints below — no personnel enter the stope.

**Answer.** Spherical charges in downholes blasting upward-retreating slices.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Increased dilution in an underground metal mine
options: raises the head grade and lowers milling cost
lowers the head grade and raises processing cost per unit of metal produced
has no effect on plant performance
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dilution adds barren tonnage.

**Reasoning.** Mixing waste with ore reduces the feed grade and forces the plant to treat more tonnes per unit of contained metal, increasing unit processing cost and possibly reducing recovery.

**Answer.** Lower head grade, higher processing cost per unit metal.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Which of the following are caving methods of underground mining?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Caving methods deliberately induce the rock mass to collapse.

**Reasoning.** Block caving, sublevel caving and top slicing all rely on caving of ore and/or overlying waste. Cut-and-fill is a supported (filled) method, not a caving one.

**Answer.** Block caving, sublevel caving and top slicing.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Which of the following are naturally supported (open) methods that require strong ore and/or wall rock?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Open methods leave the opening self-supporting during mining.

**Reasoning.** Sublevel stoping, shrinkage stoping and room-and-pillar all rely on the inherent strength of the ground (and/or pillars/broken ore). Cut-and-fill is an artificially supported method using backfill.

**Answer.** Sublevel stoping, shrinkage stoping and room-and-pillar.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Room-and-pillar mining of a metalliferous deposit is most appropriate for
options: flat-lying or gently dipping tabular deposits at moderate depth, with pillars left for roof support
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Room-and-pillar suits bedded, low-dip deposits.

**Reasoning.** Rooms are mined leaving regular pillars to hold the roof; the geometry works for flat/gently dipping tabular orebodies (e.g. limestone, salt, some base metals) where depth/stress allow economic pillar sizes.

**Answer.** Flat-lying tabular deposits at moderate depth.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In a metal room-and-pillar layout, square pillars 12 m wide are formed with 6 m rooms (18 m centres). The development extraction ratio is ____ (four decimals).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An orebody holds 2 Mt in situ; with partial pillar recovery on retreat the overall recovery is 70%. The recoverable ore, in Mt, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Recoverable $=$ in-situ $\times$ recovery.

**Working.**
$$R=2\times0.70=1.4\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 1.4 Mt.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The choice of an underground mining method depends primarily on
options: the colour of the ore only
orebody geometry (size, shape, dip, depth), ground conditions, grade/value, and dilution/recovery economics
the distance to the nearest town
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Method selection is a multi-factor engineering decision.

**Reasoning.** Geometry sets which methods are geometrically feasible; ore and wall strength set the support strategy; grade/value and the dilution/recovery balance set the economics. Production rate and capital also matter.

**Answer.** Geometry, ground conditions, grade/value and dilution/recovery.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For a high-grade, irregular orebody with weak ore and weak walls, the most suitable method is generally
options: cut-and-fill (or square-set) stoping, which supports the walls and limits dilution
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Weak ground plus high grade demands tight control.

**Reasoning.** Cut-and-fill supports the weak walls with backfill and follows irregular boundaries selectively, minimising dilution of valuable ore — justifying its higher cost for high-grade orebodies.

**Answer.** Cut-and-fill / square-set stoping.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For a large, massive, low-grade orebody where surface subsidence is acceptable, the lowest-cost method is usually
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Low grade demands the cheapest possible mining.

**Reasoning.** Block caving uses gravity to break and move ore at very low operating cost per tonne, making low-grade massive deposits economic where caving/subsidence is permissible.

**Answer.** Block caving.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: If block caving costs $5/t and cut-and-fill costs $30/t, the cut-and-fill cost is how many times the block-caving cost? ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ratio of unit costs.

**Working.**
$$\frac{30}{5}=6$$

**Answer.** 6 times.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Drawpoint spacing in a caving mine must be chosen so that
options: draw zones do not interact at all
the draw columns interact to give uniform draw, minimising ore loss and waste dilution
the spacing equals the shaft diameter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Draw geometry controls recovery and dilution.

**Reasoning.** Spacing matched to fragmentation lets adjacent draw ellipsoids overlap (interactive draw), giving a near-uniform descent of the ore-waste interface; too-wide spacing leaves pillars of ore and lets waste finger down early.

**Answer.** So draw columns interact for uniform draw.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A caving mine has 50 active drawpoints, each drawing 500 t/day. The daily production, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ drawpoints $\times$ tonnage per drawpoint.

**Working.**
$$Q=50\times500=25{,}000\ \text{t/day}$$

**Answer.** 25,000 t/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Undercutting in block caving is performed to
options: remove a horizontal slice beneath the block, withdrawing support so that the block begins to cave
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Caving must be initiated by removing the floor support.

**Reasoning.** A continuous undercut is blasted at the base of the block; once support is gone, the overlying ore loses confinement and caves progressively upward under gravity and induced stress.

**Answer.** Remove a basal slice to initiate caving.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A caving block has a 100 m × 100 m footprint and a 150 m ore column; ore density is 2.7 t/m³ and recovery is 90%. The recoverable ore, in Mt, is ____ (three decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Recoverable $=$ volume $\times$ density $\times$ recovery.

**Working.**
$$V=100\times100\times150=1.5\times10^6\ \text{m}^3$$
$$R=1.5\times10^6\times2.7\times0.9=3.645\times10^6\ \text{t}=3.645\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 3.645 Mt.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A drum winder raises the conveyance by
options: friction between rope and a fixed sheave
winding the head rope onto a rotating cylindrical drum
a rack-and-pinion on the shaft wall
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Drum winders store the rope on a drum.

**Reasoning.** The rope is physically coiled onto a cylindrical drum; winding depth is limited by drum rope capacity. Double-drum winders allow balanced hoisting of two conveyances.

**Answer.** Rope wound onto a rotating drum.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A friction (Koepe) winder drives the rope by
options: friction over a driving sheave, with a tail rope balancing the head rope; suited to deep shafts
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The Koepe principle uses traction, not storage.

**Reasoning.** The rope passes over a friction sheave with conveyances on each side; a tail rope balances the head-rope weight. With no drum to store rope, it suits great depths and shares load across multiple ropes.

**Answer.** Friction over a sheave with a balancing tail rope.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A skip of dead weight 5 t carries a 10 t payload. Ignoring rope weight and acceleration, the static rope tension, in kN, is ____ (two decimals). Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Static tension $=$ (dead + payload) $\times g$.

**Working.**
$$T=(5000+10000)\times9.81=147{,}150\ \text{N}=147.15\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 147.15 kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In a balanced two-skip winder, the two empty skips and ropes balance each other. The net out-of-balance load due to a 10 t payload only, in kN, is ____ (two decimals). Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** With balanced conveyances the winder need only lift the payload imbalance.

**Working.**
$$F=10000\times9.81=98{,}100\ \text{N}=98.1\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 98.1 kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A winder hoists a 10 t payload through 600 m in 60 s at steady speed. Ignoring losses and balance, the average power, in kW, is ____. Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Power $=\dfrac{mgh}{t}$.

**Working.**
$$P=\frac{10000\times9.81\times600}{60}=981{,}000\ \text{W}=981\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 981 kW.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: If the conveyance travels 600 m in 60 s at uniform speed, the rope (hoisting) speed, in m/s, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Speed $=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$.

**Working.**
$$v=\frac{600}{60}=10\ \text{m/s}$$

**Answer.** 10 m/s.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In shaft hoisting, a skip is used mainly to
options: transport men and materials in a floored container
hoist bulk mineral, discharged by tipping or a bottom door
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Skips are bulk-handling conveyances.

**Reasoning.** A skip is a steel box that loads ore/waste at a measuring pocket and discharges it automatically at the top; men, materials and mine cars are carried in cages instead.

**Answer.** Hoisting bulk mineral.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The tail (balance) rope hung beneath the conveyances of a friction winder
options: balances the changing weight of head rope as the conveyances move, keeping winder torque nearly constant
questions.solution:
**Concept.** As one conveyance rises, the head rope on its side shortens while the other lengthens, creating an unbalanced rope weight.

**Reasoning.** A tail rope of equal mass hung in a loop beneath the conveyances compensates this changing head-rope weight, so the out-of-balance torque (and motor load) stays nearly constant through the wind.

**Answer.** Balances head-rope weight for constant torque.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A winding rope has a breaking load of 800 kN and carries a working load of 160 kN. Its factor of safety is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FS=\dfrac{\text{breaking load}}{\text{working load}}$.

**Working.**
$$FS=\frac{800}{160}=5$$

**Answer.** 5.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A winding rope is 650 m long with a linear mass of 4 kg/m. Its total mass, in kg, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mass $=$ length $\times$ linear mass.

**Working.**
$$m=650\times4=2600\ \text{kg}$$

**Answer.** 2600 kg.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A skip (5 t) carrying 10 t payload hangs on a 2600 kg rope. The total suspended weight, in kN, is ____ (two decimals). Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Weight $=$ (skip + payload + rope mass) $\times g$.

**Working.**
$$m=5000+10000+2600=17{,}600\ \text{kg}$$
$$W=17{,}600\times9.81=172{,}656\ \text{N}=172.66\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 172.66 kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An overwind/overspeed protection device (e.g. a Lilly controller) on a winder
options: prevents the conveyance from being wound past its safe limit or exceeding safe speed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Winding safety devices guard against the most dangerous winder faults.

**Reasoning.** Overwind (winding the conveyance into the headgear) and overspeed are catastrophic; the controller monitors position/speed and trips the brakes if safe limits are approached.

**Answer.** Prevents overwind/overspeed.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A skip hoists 10 t per wind and completes one wind every 120 s. The hourly hoisting capacity, in t/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Capacity $=$ payload $\times$ winds per hour.

**Working.**
$$\text{winds/h}=\frac{3600}{120}=30,\quad Q=10\times30=300\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 300 t/h.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Operating 20 h/day at 300 t/h, the daily hoisting output, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Daily $=$ hourly rate $\times$ hours.

**Working.**
$$Q=300\times20=6000\ \text{t/day}$$

**Answer.** 6000 t/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Multi-rope friction winders are favoured for very deep shafts because
options: the load is shared among several ropes, allowing smaller rope diameter and compact, high-capacity deep winding
they eliminate the need for braking
they avoid the use of any sheave
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Spreading load across ropes overcomes single-rope limits.

**Reasoning.** At great depth a single rope would be impractically heavy/large; sharing the load over 4–6 ropes keeps each rope small and the traction sheave compact, enabling deep, high-capacity hoisting.

**Answer.** Load shared across ropes for compact deep winding.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An LHD (load-haul-dump) unit
options: is a fixed conveyor for coal
is a trackless machine that loads broken ore, trams it a short distance and dumps it, widely used in metal mines and declines
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The LHD combines loader and short-haul truck.

**Reasoning.** A low-profile, articulated, rubber-tyred machine scoops muck at the drawpoint/face, trams it to an orepass or truck, and dumps — ideal for trackless underground metal mining and decline access.

**Answer.** Trackless load-haul-dump machine.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A side-discharge loader (SDL) differs from an LHD in that it is
options: larger and used only for shaft sinking
a smaller, side-discharging unit commonly used for loading in bord-and-pillar coal development
questions.solution:
**Concept.** SDLs are compact development loaders.

**Reasoning.** The SDL gathers coal and discharges to the side onto a conveyor/shuttle, suited to the lower headroom and layout of bord-and-pillar development; LHDs are larger, front-dumping machines for metal mines.

**Answer.** A smaller side-discharge loader for coal development.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An LHD has a 4 m³ bucket at 0.9 fill factor and a cycle time of 90 s. Its loose output, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ bucket $\times$ fill $\times$ cycles/h.

**Working.**
$$\text{cycles/h}=\frac{3600}{90}=40$$
$$Q=4\times0.9\times40=144\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 144 m³/h (loose).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: If the loose ore density is 1.8 t/m³, the LHD output of 144 m³/h corresponds to ____ t/h.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ volume rate $\times$ loose density.

**Working.**
$$Q=144\times1.8=259.2\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 259.2 t/h.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A shuttle car in a bord-and-pillar coal mine
options: is a rubber-tyred car that carries coal from the continuous miner to a feeder/conveyor
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Shuttle cars bridge the face to the main transport.

**Reasoning.** They shuttle back and forth between the continuous miner and the section feeder-breaker/belt, providing flexible, trackless face haulage in room-and-pillar mining.

**Answer.** Carries coal from continuous miner to feeder/conveyor.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Belt conveyors are used underground principally for
options: continuous main-trunk haulage of coal/ore along roadways within gradient limits
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Conveyors give continuous, high-capacity transport.

**Reasoning.** Along developed roadways (within a limited gradient) belts move large, steady tonnages from the sections to the shaft/surface at low operating cost — the backbone of mechanised coal haulage.

**Answer.** Continuous trunk haulage along roadways.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A locomotive hauls a train of 20 mine cars (tubs), each loaded with 2 t. The total payload hauled, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Payload $=$ number of tubs $\times$ load per tub.

**Working.**
$$W=20\times2=40\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 40 t.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The 40 t train climbs a gradient of 1 in 100. The grade-resistance force, in kN, is ____ (two decimals). Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Grade resistance $=$ gradient $\times W g$.

**Working.**
$$F=\frac{1}{100}\times40000\times9.81=3924\ \text{N}=3.92\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 3.92 kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A tippler (rotary dumper) in a rail haulage system is used to
options: load the mine cars at the face
discharge mine cars by rotating/tipping them at the unloading point
couple the locomotive to the train
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bulk cars must be emptied mechanically.

**Reasoning.** A tippler rotates the car (or its body) to dump the contents into a bunker/skip-loading pocket, allowing rapid, continuous unloading of rail-borne mineral.

**Answer.** Discharges cars by tipping/rotating them.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Underground mine water is generally handled by
options: letting it accumulate at the face
collecting it in sumps/lodgements and pumping it to surface, often in stages
blasting it out with the ore
absorbing it into the backfill only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Water gravitates to low points and is lifted out.

**Reasoning.** Drainage channels lead water to sumps (lodgements) at shaft/winze bottoms, from where pumps lift it to surface — directly or in stages for deep mines — keeping workings dry.

**Answer.** Sump collection and staged pumping to surface.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A pump delivers 200 m³/h of water against a head of 250 m at 70% efficiency (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²). The input power, in kW, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Input power $=\dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$, $Q$ in m³/s.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{200}{3600}=0.05556\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$P=\frac{1000\times9.81\times0.05556\times250}{0.70}=194{,}700\ \text{W}\approx194.7\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 194.7 kW.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Hydraulic/paste backfill in a metal mine is valued because it
options: raises the ore grade in situ
improves ground stability, disposes of tailings underground and permits higher overall extraction
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fill is both a structural and an environmental tool.

**Reasoning.** Placed in mined voids, fill confines walls and adjacent pillars (allowing them to be recovered), controls subsidence, and provides a route to dispose of mill tailings (especially as paste) underground.

**Answer.** Stability, tailings disposal and higher extraction.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A mined void of 50,000 m³ is filled with sand of bulk density 1.6 t/m³. The mass of sand required, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mass $=$ volume $\times$ bulk density.

**Working.**
$$m=50{,}000\times1.6=80{,}000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 80,000 t.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In the winding system shown, the drum/sheave diameter is 4 m and it rotates at 60 rev/min. The rope (conveyance) speed, in m/s, is ____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='360' height='240' fill='#ffffff'/><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='340' y2='80' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='1'/><text x='24' y='74' font-size='10' fill='#111111'>surface</text><polyline points='150,80 175,20 200,80' fill='none' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='175' cy='28' r='8' fill='none' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><text x='188' y='26' font-size='10' fill='#111111'>sheave</text><line x1='150' y1='80' x2='150' y2='232' stroke='#9ca3af'/><line x1='200' y1='80' x2='200' y2='232' stroke='#9ca3af'/><line x1='168' y1='30' x2='150' y2='95' stroke='#dc2626'/><line x1='182' y1='30' x2='200' y2='200' stroke='#dc2626'/><rect x='140' y='95' width='20' height='25' fill='#9ca3af' stroke='#1f2937'/><rect x='190' y='200' width='20' height='25' fill='#9ca3af' stroke='#1f2937'/><text x='112' y='112' font-size='10' fill='#111111'>skip up</text><text x='214' y='216' font-size='10' fill='#111111'>skip down</text></svg>
figure.caption: Balanced winder: rope over a head sheave with one conveyance rising as the other descends.

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rope speed $=\pi D N$ with $N$ in rev/s.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{60}{60}=1\ \text{rev/s}$$
$$v=\pi\times4\times1=12.57\ \text{m/s}$$

**Answer.** 12.57 m/s.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Compared with a drum winder of equivalent rope, a friction (Koepe) winder is limited in maximum payload mainly because
options: drive depends on friction grip, so excessive load would cause the rope to slip on the sheave
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Traction drive has a slip limit.

**Reasoning.** The Koepe winder transmits force only through friction between rope and sheave; if the load (or out-of-balance) exceeds the available traction, the rope slips. Payload is therefore capped by the friction grip, unlike a positively wound drum.

**Answer.** Friction grip limits load to avoid rope slip.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In the bord-and-pillar plan shown, square pillars are 20 m wide and galleries are 5 m wide (centre-to-centre spacing 25 m). The percentage extraction (first working) is _____ % (one decimal).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='240' height='175' fill='#1f2937'/><g fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'><rect x='20' y='20' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='95' y='20' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='170' y='20' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='20' y='95' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='95' y='95' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='170' y='95' width='50' height='50'/></g><text x='10' y='192' font-size='11'>pillar 20 m, gallery 5 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Bord-and-pillar layout (plan)

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Extraction ratio $e=1-\dfrac{W^2}{(W+B)^2}$ for square pillars (W = pillar side, B = gallery width).

**Working.**
$$e=1-\frac{20^2}{25^2}=1-\frac{400}{625}=1-0.64=0.36=36\%$$

**Answer.** $36\%$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the longwall panel shown, the face length is 200 m, the shearer web (depth of cut) is 0.8 m, the seam is 2 m thick and the in-situ density is $1.5\ \mathrm{t/m^3}$. The coal produced in one full web pass, in tonnes, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='220' height='120' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='150' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='6'/><line x1='250' y1='30' x2='250' y2='150' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='6'/><line x1='30' y1='90' x2='250' y2='90' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='3'/><text x='140' y='84' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626'>face (length 200 m)</text><text x='8' y='95' font-size='9'>gate</text><text x='254' y='95' font-size='9'>gate</text><text x='140' y='135' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>panel</text></svg>
figure.caption: Longwall panel (plan)

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Production $=$ face length $\times$ web $\times$ seam thickness $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=200\times0.8\times2\times1.5=480\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** $480\ \text{t}$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A square pillar has width $w=8$ m and height $h=4$ m (see figure). Using the Obert–Duvall formula $S_p=S_1\left(0.778+0.222\,\dfrac{w}{h}\right)$ with $S_1=10$ MPa, the pillar strength, in MPa, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='55' y='40' width='90' height='90' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='150' x2='145' y2='150' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='1'/><text x='100' y='166' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#2563eb'>w = 8 m</text><line x1='165' y1='40' x2='165' y2='130' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1'/><text x='170' y='90' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626'>h = 4 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Pillar of width w and height h

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A pillar (see figure) has a strength of 12 MPa and carries an induced stress of 8 MPa. Its factor of safety is _____ (one decimal).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='65' y='60' width='70' height='80' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'><line x1='75' y1='30' x2='75' y2='54' marker-end='url(#dn)'/><line x1='100' y1='30' x2='100' y2='54' marker-end='url(#dn)'/><line x1='125' y1='30' x2='125' y2='54' marker-end='url(#dn)'/></g><defs><marker id='dn' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='100' y='105' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>pillar</text><text x='100' y='20' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626'>load</text></svg>
figure.caption: Pillar under induced load

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Factor of safety $=\dfrac{\text{pillar strength}}{\text{pillar stress}}$.

**Working.**
$$FoS=\frac{12}{8}=1.5$$

**Answer.** $1.5$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the bord-and-pillar layout shown with square pillars 20 m wide separated by 5 m wide galleries, compute the extraction ratio.

figure.caption: Bord-and-pillar plan
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#e5e7eb'/><g fill='#1f2937'><rect x='30' y='30' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='110' y='30' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='30' y='110' width='50' height='50'/><rect x='110' y='110' width='50' height='50'/></g><text x='86' y='60' font-size='9' fill='#475569'>B=5</text><text x='42' y='58' font-size='9' fill='#fff'>W=20</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For square pillars, extraction ratio $e = 1 - \left(\dfrac{W}{W+B}\right)^2$.

**Working.**
$$e = 1 - \left(\frac{20}{25}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{0.36}$ (36%).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using the tributary-area method for the layout shown (vertical stress 10 MPa, pillar 20 m, gallery 5 m), compute the average pillar stress (MPa).

figure.caption: Tributary area
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#e5e7eb'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='80' height='80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='64' height='64' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='150' y='105' font-size='9' fill='#475569'>sigma_v=10</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tributary area: $\sigma_p = \sigma_v \left(\dfrac{W+B}{W}\right)^2$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_p = 10 \left(\frac{25}{20}\right)^2 = 10 \times 1.5625 = 15.625$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{15.625}$ MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A pillar has a strength of 40 MPa and carries an average stress of 16 MPa (as shown). Compute its factor of safety.

figure.caption: Pillar loading
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='50' width='60' height='90' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'><line x1='60' y1='30' x2='60' y2='48' marker-end='url(#pl)'/><line x1='80' y1='30' x2='80' y2='48' marker-end='url(#pl)'/><line x1='100' y1='30' x2='100' y2='48' marker-end='url(#pl)'/></g><defs><marker id='pl' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='30' y='160' font-size='8'>strength 40, stress 16 MPa</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $FoS = \dfrac{\text{strength}}{\text{stress}}$.

**Working.**
$$FoS = \frac{40}{16} = 2.5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{2.5}$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the longwall face shown (face length 200 m, web depth 0.8 m, seam height 2 m, coal density 1.4 t/m³), compute the coal produced per shear (tonnes).

figure.caption: Longwall face
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='100' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='14' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='120' y='40' font-size='8' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle'>face L=200 m</text><rect x='30' y='44' width='12' height='86' fill='#94a3b8'/><text x='60' y='85' font-size='8'>web 0.8 m, h=2 m</text><text x='60' y='105' font-size='8'>rho=1.4 t/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage per shear = face length × web × height × density.

**Working.**
$$T = 200 \times 0.8 \times 2 \times 1.4 = 448$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{448}$ t per shear.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: If the longwall shown advances by one 0.8 m web per shear and takes 10 shears per day, compute the daily face advance (m/day).

figure.caption: Longwall advance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='14' height='70' fill='#94a3b8'/><line x1='44' y1='115' x2='62' y2='115' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#aa)'/><defs><marker id='aa' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='70' y='119' font-size='8'>web 0.8 m, 10 shears/day</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Daily advance = web × number of shears.

**Working.**
$$A = 0.8 \times 10 = 8$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{8}$ m/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A development panel is 200 m wide and pillars are laid out on 25 m centres (as shown). How many pillar centres span the panel width?

figure.caption: Pillar row
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 250 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#1f2937'><rect x='20' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='48' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='76' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='104' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='132' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='160' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='188' y='40' width='18' height='30'/><rect x='216' y='40' width='18' height='30'/></g><text x='60' y='90' font-size='8'>panel 200 m, centres 25 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of pillar centres = panel width ÷ centre spacing.

**Working.**
$$N = \frac{200}{25} = 8$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{8}$ pillars.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A single-rope winder lifts a cage of mass 10 t carrying a 5 t load (as shown). Taking g = 9.81 m/s², compute the static rope tension (kN), ignoring rope weight.

figure.caption: Winding headgear
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='100' cy='40' r='18' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='100' y1='58' x2='100' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='85' y='150' width='30' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><text x='122' y='166' font-size='8'>cage 10 t</text><text x='122' y='180' font-size='8'>load 5 t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Static tension = total suspended mass × g.

**Working.**
$$T = (10000 + 5000)(9.81) = 15000 \times 9.81 = 147150\ \text{N} = 147.15\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{147.15}$ kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In the balanced (two-cage) winder shown, the cage masses balance each other. Compute the out-of-balance force (kN) due to a 10 t payload alone (g = 9.81 m/s²).

figure.caption: Balanced winder
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='110' cy='35' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='95' y1='45' x2='95' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='125' y1='45' x2='125' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='150' width='30' height='28' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='112' y='120' width='28' height='26' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='52' y='196' font-size='8'>balanced winder, payload 10 t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** With cages balanced, only the payload weight is unbalanced.

**Working.**
$$F = 10000 \times 9.81 = 98100\ \text{N} = 98.1\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{98.1}$ kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Roof bolts are installed on a 1.25 m × 1.25 m square pattern as shown. Compute the bolt density (bolts per m²).

figure.caption: Roof bolt grid
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='140' height='140' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='50' cy='50' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='50' r='4'/><circle cx='130' cy='50' r='4'/><circle cx='50' cy='90' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='90' r='4'/><circle cx='130' cy='90' r='4'/><circle cx='50' cy='130' r='4'/><circle cx='90' cy='130' r='4'/><circle cx='130' cy='130' r='4'/></g><text x='32' y='175' font-size='8'>spacing 1.25 m square</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bolt density = 1 ÷ (spacing)² for a square grid.

**Working.**
$$\rho = \frac{1}{1.25^2} = \frac{1}{1.5625} = 0.64$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{0.64}$ bolts/m².

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A roof bolt must suspend the loose rock block shown (1.5 m × 1.5 m plan, 1 m thick, unit weight 25 kN/m³). Compute the dead load (kN) carried by one bolt supporting this block.

figure.caption: Suspension bolting
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='160' height='30' fill='#a16207'/><rect x='30' y='60' width='160' height='40' fill='#d6c0a3' stroke='#92400e'/><text x='70' y='85' font-size='8'>loose rock block</text><line x1='110' y1='100' x2='110' y2='45' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='3'/><text x='34' y='130' font-size='8'>block 1.5x1.5x1 m, 25 kN/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dead load = unit weight × block volume.

**Working.**
$$W = 25 \times (1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1) = 25 \times 2.25 = 56.25$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{56.25}$ kN.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A heading is driven with 3 blasting rounds per day, each pulling 1.5 m (as shown). Compute the daily drivage rate (m/day).

figure.caption: Heading advance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,90 30,40 60,30 90,40 90,90' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='90' y1='65' x2='190' y2='65' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3' marker-end='url(#da)'/><defs><marker id='da' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='44' y='110' font-size='8'>3 rounds/day, pull 1.5 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Drivage rate = rounds per day × pull per round.

**Working.**
$$R = 3 \times 1.5 = 4.5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4.5}$ m/day.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: An inclined drift reaches a vertical depth of 100 m at an inclination of 30° to the horizontal (as shown). Compute the length of the drift (m).

figure.caption: Inclined drift
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='200' y1='30' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='205' y='85' font-size='8'>depth 100 m</text><text x='66' y='72' font-size='8' transform='rotate(29 66 72)'>30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vertical depth = drift length × sin(inclination).

**Working.**
$$L = \frac{100}{\sin 30^\circ} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{200}$ m.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For a layout of 15 m square pillars separated by 5 m galleries (as shown), compute the extraction ratio.

figure.caption: Pillar plan
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#e5e7eb'/><g fill='#1f2937'><rect x='35' y='35' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='115' y='35' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='35' y='115' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='115' y='115' width='45' height='45'/></g><text x='88' y='62' font-size='9' fill='#475569'>B=5</text><text x='44' y='62' font-size='9' fill='#fff'>W=15</text></svg>

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: For the same 15 m pillars with 5 m galleries (as shown) under a vertical stress of 12 MPa, compute the average pillar stress (MPa) by the tributary-area method.

figure.caption: Tributary area
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#e5e7eb'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='80' height='80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='60' height='60' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='150' y='105' font-size='9' fill='#475569'>sigma_v=12</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_p = \sigma_v \left(\dfrac{W+B}{W}\right)^2$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_p = 12 \left(\frac{20}{15}\right)^2 = 12 \times 1.7778 = 21.33$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{21.33}$ MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Compute the vertical in-situ stress (MPa) at the depth shown (H = 400 m, unit weight 25 kN/m³).

figure.caption: Overburden column
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='50' y='20' width='60' height='130' fill='#d6c0a3' stroke='#92400e'/><line x1='80' y1='20' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><text x='28' y='170' font-size='8'>H=400 m, gamma=25 kN/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vertical stress $\sigma_v = \gamma H$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_v = 25 \times 400 = 10000\ \text{kPa} = 10\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{10}$ MPa.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The layout shown — coal extracted in galleries leaving regular square pillars to support the roof — is which method?

figure.caption: Method plan
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#e5e7eb'/><g fill='#1f2937'><rect x='30' y='30' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='115' y='30' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='30' y='115' width='45' height='45'/><rect x='115' y='115' width='45' height='45'/></g></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Regular pillars left between driven galleries characterise bord-and-pillar mining.

**Reasoning.** The grid of square coal pillars supporting the roof while galleries are driven around them is the defining geometry of the bord-and-pillar (room-and-pillar) method.

**Answer.** Bord-and-pillar (room-and-pillar).

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In the method shown, a long face is supported by powered chock shields and the roof behind the supports is allowed to cave. This is:

figure.caption: Long face with caving
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='150' height='80' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='12' height='80' fill='#94a3b8'/><g fill='#fca5a5'><rect x='185' y='30' width='10' height='12'/><rect x='200' y='45' width='10' height='12'/><rect x='190' y='60' width='10' height='12'/></g><text x='185' y='130' font-size='8'>goaf (caved)</text><text x='50' y='128' font-size='8'>powered supports</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A continuous supported face with controlled caving behind is longwall mining.

**Reasoning.** Powered roof supports advancing along a long face while the goaf caves behind them is the signature of longwall mining with caving.

**Answer.** Longwall mining with caving.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The wide barrier (chain) pillar shown between two longwall panels is left primarily to:

figure.caption: Barrier pillar
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='80' height='70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='100' y='30' width='40' height='70' fill='#1f2937'/><rect x='140' y='30' width='80' height='70' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><text x='102' y='118' font-size='8'>barrier pillar</text></svg>

options: isolate and protect the adjacent panel/roadways from abutment loads
act as the main ventilation fan
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Barrier (chain) pillars separate workings and carry abutment stress.

**Reasoning.** A wide barrier pillar shields the neighbouring panel and its roadways from the front and side abutment loads of the adjacent extracted panel, protecting them from convergence and inrush.

**Answer.** Isolate and protect the adjacent panel/roadways from abutment loads.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The self-advancing support shown on a longwall face, which holds the roof and pushes the conveyor forward, is a:

figure.caption: Powered support
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='30' width='120' height='12' fill='#475569'/><rect x='40' y='118' width='120' height='12' fill='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='42' x2='70' y2='118' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='6'/><line x1='130' y1='42' x2='130' y2='118' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='6'/><text x='52' y='150' font-size='8'>hydraulic chock shield</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Longwall faces use self-advancing hydraulic powered supports.
